meta.title: E2E Testing
meta.description: Cypress, Playwright, Selenium end-to-end testing patterns and best practices

items.text: Are stable selectors used (data-testid, not CSS classes or XPath)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use data-testid or data-cy attributes for selectors. Don't rely on CSS classes, tag names, or XPath that change with UI redesigns.
baseContent.whyItMatters: CSS class selectors break when styles change. XPath breaks when the DOM structure changes. data-testid survives both.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for selectors using CSS classes (.btn-primary), tag paths (div > span), or XPath. These should use data-testid.
baseContent.exampleComment: cy.get('.btn.btn-primary.submit') breaks when button styles change. Use cy.get('[data-testid="submit-button"]') instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use data-testid for E2E selectors. They're immune to style and structure changes.

items.text: Are page objects or component abstractions used to encapsulate UI interactions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Wrap page interactions in Page Object classes or custom commands. Tests should read like user stories, not DOM manipulation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without page objects, selector changes require updating every test. Page objects centralize selectors and interactions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for raw selectors scattered across test files. These should be in page object methods.
baseContent.exampleComment: Every test repeats cy.get('[data-testid=email]').type() and cy.get('[data-testid=password]').type(). Create a LoginPage.login(email, pass) method.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Encapsulate UI interactions in page objects. Tests should read as user workflows.

items.text: Are selectors free of position-based or index-based targeting?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Don't select elements by position (:nth-child, [0], first()). Adding an item before the target breaks the selector.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Index-based selectors are the #1 cause of flaky E2E tests. Any change to the list order breaks them.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for :nth-child, .eq(), [index], .first(), .last() in selectors. Replace with data-testid or content-based selectors.
baseContent.exampleComment: cy.get('tr').eq(2) breaks when a new row is added. Use cy.get('[data-testid="order-row-123"]') with a unique identifier.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use index-based selectors. Use unique identifiers via data-testid.

items.text: Are accessibility-friendly selectors preferred (roles, labels, text)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Prefer selectors based on accessible roles and labels: getByRole, getByLabelText, getByText. This tests accessibility too.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Accessibility-based selectors verify that the UI is accessible while testing functionality. Two birds, one stone.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if tests could use role-based selectors instead of data-testid. Verify buttons have accessible names.
baseContent.exampleComment: Instead of data-testid='submit', use getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit Order' }) to test accessibility simultaneously.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer accessibility selectors (getByRole, getByLabelText) when possible.

items.text: Are selectors defined in one place (not hardcoded throughout tests)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Define selectors as constants or in page objects. Don't repeat the same selector string in multiple test files.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When a selector changes, you update it in one place instead of finding every occurrence across all test files.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for the same data-testid string appearing in multiple files. Centralize in page objects or constants.
baseContent.exampleComment: '[data-testid=user-menu]' appears in 8 test files. Define it once in a shared constants file or page object.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define selectors once. Reuse through page objects or constants.

items.text: Are hard waits (sleep, cy.wait(ms)) avoided?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Never use cy.wait(3000), page.waitForTimeout(), or Thread.sleep(). Use smart waits that wait for specific conditions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hard waits are too slow (waiting 3s when 100ms suffices) or too fast (3s isn't enough on slow CI). They make tests slow and flaky.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for cy.wait(number), waitForTimeout, sleep, Thread.sleep in E2E tests. Replace with condition-based waits.
baseContent.exampleComment: cy.wait(5000) before asserting. Use cy.get('[data-testid=result]').should('be.visible') which auto-retries until visible.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use hard waits. Wait for specific conditions (visible, contains, network idle).

items.text: Are network requests properly awaited (not assumed complete)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Wait for specific API responses before asserting. Use cy.intercept().as('alias') + cy.wait('@alias') or page.waitForResponse().
baseContent.whyItMatters: Asserting before API responses arrive causes flaky failures. The data hasn't loaded yet when the assertion runs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that actions triggering API calls wait for the response before asserting on the result.
baseContent.exampleComment: This clicks Save and immediately asserts the success message. Intercept the POST request and wait for its response first.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Intercept and wait for API responses. Don't assert before data has loaded.

items.text: Are assertions using auto-retry mechanisms (should, expect with retry)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use framework retry mechanisms: Cypress .should() retries automatically, Playwright expect() has auto-retry, Testing Library waitFor().
baseContent.whyItMatters: Single-shot assertions fail on slow renders. Auto-retry assertions wait for the condition to become true.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that assertions on dynamic content use retrying mechanisms, not one-shot checks.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses a plain expect() which doesn't retry. Use await expect(locator).toBeVisible() in Playwright for auto-retry.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use auto-retrying assertions for dynamic content. Single-shot assertions cause flakiness.

items.text: Are animations and transitions handled before interactions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Wait for animations to complete before clicking or asserting. Or disable animations in test mode entirely.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Clicking an element during an animation can miss the target. Assertions during transitions see intermediate states.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for flaky click tests. Verify the app disables animations in test mode or tests wait for animation completion.
baseContent.exampleComment: This click fails intermittently because the dropdown is still animating. Add { animationDistanceThreshold: 0 } or disable animations.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Disable animations in test mode or wait for them to complete before interacting.

items.text: Are timeouts configured appropriately (not too short, not too long)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Set default timeouts appropriate for CI speed. Use longer timeouts for specific slow operations, not globally.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Default 4s timeout is too short for CI. Setting 30s globally makes failures take forever to surface.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check timeout configuration. Verify slow operations have specific longer timeouts, not a blanket increase.
baseContent.exampleComment: Global timeout is 30s, making failures take 30s to report. Use 10s default with 30s only for file upload tests.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set reasonable default timeouts. Use longer timeouts only for specific slow operations.

items.text: Does each test create its own test data (not sharing across tests)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each test should set up the data it needs via API calls or database seeds. Don't rely on data from previous tests.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Shared test data causes cascading failures. If the 'create user' test fails, every subsequent test that needs that user also fails.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if tests depend on data created by other tests. Verify each test can run in isolation.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test assumes a user 'testuser@example.com' exists. Create the user in a beforeEach hook via the API.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Each test creates its own data. No dependencies between tests.

items.text: Is test data cleaned up after tests (no leftover state)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Delete or reset test data after each test or test suite. Use database transactions, API cleanup, or fresh environments.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Accumulated test data slows subsequent runs, causes unique constraint violations, and makes debugging harder.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for afterEach/afterAll cleanup. Verify the database is clean between test runs. Check for unique constraint errors.
baseContent.exampleComment: Tests create users without cleanup. After 100 runs, the database has thousands of test users. Add afterEach cleanup.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Clean up test data. Use database reset, API deletion, or transactional rollback.

items.text: Is test data seeded via API (not through the UI)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Set up test preconditions using API calls or direct database commands, not by clicking through the UI.
baseContent.whyItMatters: UI-based setup is slow (minutes vs seconds) and fragile. If the registration form changes, all tests that create users break.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if tests use UI interactions for setup. Replace with API calls: cy.request(), page.request, or custom commands.
baseContent.exampleComment: Each test fills out the registration form to create a user. Use cy.request('POST', '/api/users', userData) instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Seed data via API. Only use the UI for the specific workflow being tested.

items.text: Are flaky tests identified and fixed (not silently retried)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Don't hide flakiness with automatic retries. Track flaky tests, fix root causes (timing, data, selectors).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Retrying hides real bugs. A test that passes on retry 2 of 3 has a real problem — timing issue, race condition, or data leak.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for retry configuration. If retries > 0, verify there's a process to track and fix flaky tests.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test has retries: 3 configured. Instead of retrying, fix the root cause: add a proper wait for the API response.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Fix flaky tests, don't retry them. Retries mask real problems.

items.text: Can tests run in any order and in parallel?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each test must be completely independent. No test should assume another test ran first or set up state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Order-dependent tests break parallel execution and cause mysterious CI failures when test order changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run a single test in isolation. If it fails, it has hidden dependencies on other tests.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test passes only when run after 'should create order'. It depends on the order existing. Add its own order creation.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every test is self-contained. Run alone, run in parallel, run in any order.

items.text: Do E2E tests cover complete user workflows (not isolated clicks)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: E2E tests should cover full user journeys: login -> search -> add to cart -> checkout. Not isolated button clicks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: E2E tests are expensive. Use them for complete workflows that verify integration. Unit tests cover isolated behavior.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that each E2E test represents a real user workflow from start to finish. Isolated interactions belong in unit tests.
baseContent.exampleComment: This E2E test only clicks a button and checks it's disabled. That's a unit test. E2E should test the full purchase flow.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: E2E tests = user workflows. Use unit tests for isolated behaviors.

items.text: Is the test count appropriate (fewer E2E tests, more unit tests)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Follow the test pyramid: many unit tests, some integration tests, few E2E tests. E2E tests should cover critical paths only.
baseContent.whyItMatters: E2E tests are slow and expensive. 500 E2E tests take hours. Cover edge cases with unit tests, critical paths with E2E.
baseContent.howToVerify: Count E2E vs unit tests. If E2E outnumber unit tests, the pyramid is inverted. Move non-critical tests to unit level.
baseContent.exampleComment: There are 200 E2E tests and 50 unit tests. The pyramid is inverted. Move validation and edge case tests to unit level.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow the test pyramid. E2E for critical paths, unit tests for everything else.

items.text: Are visual regression tests used for UI-critical components?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use visual regression testing (Percy, Chromatic, Playwright screenshots) to catch unintended visual changes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Functional tests don't catch visual regressions: misaligned text, broken layouts, wrong colors. Screenshots do.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for screenshot comparison tests on critical UI components. Verify they run on consistent viewport sizes.
baseContent.exampleComment: This checkout page redesign has no visual regression test. Add Percy or Playwright screenshot tests for the checkout flow.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add visual regression tests for critical UI. Screenshots catch what functional tests miss.

items.text: Are tests run across required browsers/viewports?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Run E2E tests on all supported browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, mobile). Verify responsive behavior at key breakpoints.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Browser differences cause real bugs. A feature working in Chrome but broken in Safari affects 25% of mobile users.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CI configuration for multi-browser testing. Verify mobile viewports are tested.
baseContent.exampleComment: Tests only run in Chrome. Add Firefox and WebKit (Safari) to the Playwright configuration to catch browser-specific bugs.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test on all supported browsers. Include mobile viewports for responsive testing.

items.text: Are error states and edge cases tested (network failure, timeouts)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test what users see when things go wrong: network failures, 500 errors, timeouts, empty states, unauthorized access.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users encounter errors frequently. If the error UI is broken, users see blank screens instead of helpful messages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for tests that simulate network failures, slow responses, and error responses. Verify error UI is tested.
baseContent.exampleComment: No tests for what happens when the API returns 500. Intercept the request and return an error to test the error state.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test error scenarios: network failures, timeouts, server errors. Users see these regularly.

items.text: Is authentication handled efficiently (not logging in through UI every test)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Set authentication state programmatically (inject cookies, localStorage tokens) instead of filling the login form every test.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Logging in through the UI in every test adds 5-10 seconds per test. With 100 tests, that's 15+ minutes of just login.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check how tests authenticate. If every test fills the login form, switch to programmatic auth setup.
baseContent.exampleComment: Every test navigates to /login and types credentials. Use cy.setCookie() or storageState to skip the login UI.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Authenticate programmatically. Only test the login UI in the login test itself.

items.text: Are E2E tests stable in CI (not frequently failing)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: E2E tests in CI should have a >98% pass rate. Frequent flaky failures erode trust and cause developers to ignore failures.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A test suite that fails randomly is worse than no tests. Developers learn to ignore failures and real bugs slip through.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CI pass rates for E2E tests over the past month. Identify and fix tests with <95% pass rate.
baseContent.exampleComment: E2E tests fail 3 times a week on CI. Track the flaky tests, fix root causes (timing, data), and monitor pass rates.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Maintain >98% CI pass rate. Fix flaky tests immediately, don't just re-run.

items.text: Are failure artifacts captured (screenshots, videos, traces)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Configure test runners to capture screenshots on failure, record videos, and save trace files for debugging.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without artifacts, debugging CI failures requires reproducing locally. Screenshots and videos show exactly what went wrong.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CI configuration for artifact uploading. Verify screenshots/videos are captured on failure.
baseContent.exampleComment: E2E failures in CI have no screenshots. Enable screenshot-on-failure and upload as CI artifacts.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Capture screenshots and videos on failure. Upload as CI artifacts for easy debugging.

items.text: Are tests parallelized for faster CI execution?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Split E2E tests across multiple CI workers/containers for parallel execution. Use sharding or spec-level parallelism.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Serial E2E execution can take 30+ minutes. Parallel execution across 4 workers cuts this to 8 minutes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CI configuration for parallel workers. Verify tests are evenly distributed across workers.
baseContent.exampleComment: 100 E2E tests run serially in 40 minutes. Configure 4 parallel workers with Playwright sharding to run in 10 minutes.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Parallelize E2E tests in CI. Use sharding to distribute across workers.

items.text: Does the test environment match production (not a mock-everything setup)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: E2E tests should run against a real backend, real database, and real (or realistic) third-party integrations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: E2E tests against mocked backends don't test integration. They become expensive unit tests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check what the E2E tests connect to. Verify it's a real application stack, not a mock server.
baseContent.exampleComment: E2E tests run against a mock API. They don't catch backend bugs. Set up a real test environment with Docker Compose.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: E2E tests need a real backend. Mock only external third-party services.

items.text: Are test results reported in a readable format (not raw console output)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use structured test reporters (JUnit XML, HTML report, Allure) that integrate with CI dashboards.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Raw console output is hard to parse for failures. Structured reports show pass/fail at a glance with failure details.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for test reporter configuration. Verify CI shows test results in a dashboard, not raw logs.
baseContent.exampleComment: Test results are only in console output. Add JUnit reporter for CI integration and HTML reporter for local debugging.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use structured test reporters. Integrate with CI dashboards for visibility.
